lessonTitle: The Lord Raised Up a Deliverer
quickInsight: Every time you fall, Christ is still ready to pull you back.

quotableVerses.text: And when the children of Israel cried unto the Lord, the Lord raised up a deliverer to the children of Israel, who delivered them, even Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother.
And Deborah said unto Barak, Up; for this is the day in which the Lord hath delivered Sisera into thine hand: is not the Lord gone out before thee?
And the Lord said unto Gideon, The people that are with thee are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands, lest Israel vaunt themselves against me, saying, Mine own hand hath saved me.
And he awoke out of his sleep, and said, I will go out as at other times before, and shake myself. And he wist not that the Lord was departed from him.

sections.title: The Story
items: Israel keeps repeating the same cycle: they forget God, chase after what everyone around them is doing, hit rock bottom, cry out for help — and God sends someone to save them. Every. Single. Time.

Deborah is a prophetess and judge who rallies a terrified general named Barak to fight an impossible battle. Her faith is so contagious she basically says, 'Get up. God's already out there ahead of you.'
Gideon has an army of 32,000, and God says 'Too many.' He cuts it down to 300 men with torches and trumpets — because God wants Israel to know HE won the battle, not them.

Samson is born with a divine calling and supernatural strength. But he keeps chasing what feels good in the moment — wrong relationships, wrong places, wrong choices — until he literally doesn't realize God's power has left him.

sections.title: The Samson Warning
items: Samson is the cautionary tale every young man needs to hear. He had more God-given potential than almost anyone in scripture — and he wasted it because he couldn't say no to what 'pleaseth him well.'

The scariest verse in Judges: 'He wist not that the Lord was departed from him.' Samson didn't even notice the Spirit was gone. That's what happens when you keep pushing boundaries — you stop feeling the difference.
Sister Dibb said it perfectly: 'Instead of arising and shining forth to fulfill his great potential, Samson was overcome by the world, lost his God-given power, and died a tragic, early death.'
You're going to face situations where the wrong choice feels exciting and the right choice feels boring. Samson picked exciting every time. Don't be Samson.

sections.title: Why This Matters to You
items: Israel's problem wasn't one big rebellion — it was slowly blending in with the people around them until they couldn't tell the difference anymore. That's how peer pressure actually works. Not one big moment, but a slow drift.

You're going to mess up the same way more than once. That's not the end of the story. The whole book of Judges proves God doesn't give up on people who keep falling — as long as they keep coming back.

Gideon's 300 soldiers prove something: God doesn't need you to be the biggest, strongest, or most popular. He needs you to show up and trust Him. Your 'not enough' is more than enough when God's in it.
Deborah's faith didn't just save her — it lifted everyone around her. Your faith at school, at practice, in your friend group matters more than you think. One person's courage can change the room.

sections.title: Talk Starters
items: Judges 2:19 says Israel was 'worse than their fathers.' What's one area where it's easy for our generation to drift further from God than the last one did?
Samson didn't even realize the Spirit had left. How do you know when you're starting to drift? What does it feel like when the Holy Ghost is pulling back?
Gideon's army went from 32,000 to 300. Has there been a time when you felt outmatched or outnumbered doing the right thing? What happened?
Deborah told Barak, 'Is not the Lord gone out before thee?' If you really believed God was already ahead of you in your hardest situation, what would you do differently?

sections.title: How to Teach It
items: Draw the cycle on the board: Forget God → Bad consequences → Cry for help → God delivers → Repeat. Ask them where they are in the cycle. Then ask: what breaks the cycle? (Answer: actually remembering — which is exactly what the sacrament is for.)

Hand a boy his phone and tell him to set it to silent without looking at the screen. Point: Samson's strength left so gradually he didn't notice. Sin works the same way — you stop feeling the warnings before you stop doing the thing.

Read Judges 7:16–21 and give them the math: 300 guys with torches vs. 135,000 soldiers. Ask: what's YOUR version of 300 vs. 135,000? Then read D&C 84:88 — 'I will go before your face. I will be on your right hand and on your left.'

sections.title: Challenge for the Week
items: Pick one cycle you're stuck in — something you keep falling back into even though you've tried to stop. This week, instead of just feeling bad about it, actually cry unto the Lord like Israel did. Kneel down and say it out loud in prayer. Name it.
Be Deborah once this week: say something about Christ or your faith to someone who needs to hear it — a friend, a sibling, someone at school. Just one real sentence of testimony.

sections.title: Restoration Connection
items: Alma 36 is the Book of Mormon's version of the Judges cycle — Alma hits absolute rock bottom, calls on Christ, and is delivered. Read Alma 36:18–21 side by side with Judges 3:9. Same pattern, same God.

D&C 84:87–88 is the answer to Deborah's question 'Is not the Lord gone out before thee?' — 'I will go before your face. I will be on your right hand and on your left, and my Spirit shall be in your hearts, and mine angels round about you.'

Elder Oaks taught that covenants carry both responsibilities and power. Samson lost his power when he broke his covenants. Your baptismal and priesthood covenants work the same way — keep them, and the power stays.
